title: Baba Ganoush (Smoky Aubergine Dip)

ingredients.name: large brinjal (aubergine / eggplant)
visual_checks: choose the fattest ones you can find — more flesh, less skin
ingredients.notes: choose the fattest ones you can find — more flesh, less skin

subs.name: this dish cannot be substituted — roasted brinjal is the dish
subs.notes: try mashed roasted capsicum for a different smoky dip

subs.name: 2 tbsp roasted sesame seeds blended with 1 tbsp oil
2 tbsp vinegar — use half the quantity
subs.notes: a last resort; lemon juice is much better here

steps.text: Place the whole brinjal directly on the gas burner over medium-high flame. Roast, turning with tongs every 3 minutes, for 12–15 minutes until the skin is completely charred and collapsed and the flesh inside is very soft. The brinjal should look burnt — this is correct.

Place the charred brinjal in a colander over a bowl and leave to steam and cool for 10 minutes — this step releases bitter juices. When cool enough to handle, peel off and discard all the charred skin.
Squeeze the peeled flesh gently in your hands to remove excess liquid. Finely chop with a knife rather than blending for a rougher, more textured dip — or blend briefly for a smoother result.

Mix the brinjal with minced garlic, tahini, lemon juice, cumin and salt. Taste and adjust lemon and salt — the dip should be assertively smoky and tart. Add a tablespoon of cold water if the consistency is too thick.
Spread into a plate, drizzle olive oil over the surface, and scatter parsley or coriander. Serve at room temperature with warm bread, pita or fresh vegetable sticks.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: tahini → 2 tbsp roasted sesame seeds blended with 1 tbsp oil (grinding fresh sesame works well); lemon → 2 tbsp vinegar — use half the quantity (a last resort; lemon juice is much better here); brinjal → this dish cannot be substituted — roasted brinjal is the dish (try mashed roasted capsicum for a different smoky dip)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This is the same technique as the North Indian baigan bharta — char the brinjal directly on your gas hob. Brinjal is inexpensive and easy to find. The only unusual ingredient is tahini — substitute with a paste of toasted til seeds blended with a little oil.
